title: 10 best herbal teas for sore eyes
intro_desc: Herbal teas for sore eyes are a natural remedy made from various plant extracts, rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, which help soothe and calm irritated eyes. These teas can provide relief from dryness, itchiness, and redness caused by fatigue, allergies, or prolonged screen use. Examples of herbal teas that help with sore eyes include chamomile, calendula, and peppermint, which can be consumed as a warm liquid or used as an eyewash. Drinking these teas regularly can improve daily life by reducing eye strain and discomfort, allowing individuals to focus on their tasks without distraction.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple coneflower teas helps with sore eyes because its natural anti-inflammatory properties soothe and calm irritated eye tissues. The tea's flavonoids and terpenes work together to reduce swelling, ease redness, and diminish discomfort associated with dry or irritated eyes. Additionally, the soothing effects of purple coneflower teas may help to relax the muscles around the eyes, providing relief from tension headaches and migraines that often accompany sore eyes.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Purple coneflower tea helps reduce inflammation and swelling in the eyes, which can alleviate redness, itching, and discomfort caused by conditions like conjunctivitis or dry eye syndrome.

Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in purple coneflower tea help neutralize free radicals that can damage eye tissues, reducing the risk of age-related macular degeneration and other vision problems.

Wound-healing properties: Purple coneflower has been shown to accelerate wound healing by promoting cell regeneration and improving blood flow, which can aid in the recovery process for eye injuries or conditions like blepharitis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the purple coneflower contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in reducing redness and swelling associated with sore eyes.
Barks: The barks of the purple coneflower contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that help to soothe and calm irritated eyes, reducing discomfort and promoting healing.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried echinacea purpurea flowers for every 8 ounces of boiling water.
Heat the water in a teapot or kettle until it reaches a rolling boil for two minutes.
Add the measured echinacea purpurea flowers to the hot water and let it steep for five to seven minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Allow the tea to cool down to lukewarm temperature before drinking it within 15 to 20 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold teas helps with sore eyes because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The tea contains flavonoids, which help to reduce redness and swelling in the eyes, providing relief from itchiness and dryness. Additionally, the tea's antiseptic properties can help to soothe and calm irritated eyes, reducing the risk of infection and promoting healthy vision. Regular consumption of pot marigold teas may also help to improve overall eye health by protecting against age-related macular degeneration and cataracts.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant Properties: Marigold tea's antioxidant compounds help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the eyes, soothing irritated or itchy eyes caused by dryness, allergies, or minor injuries.

Anti-inflammatory Properties: The flavonoids present in marigold tea possess anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce swelling and redness in the eyes, providing relief from conditions like conjunctivitis (pink eye) and other inflammatory eye disorders.

Antimicrobial Properties: Marigold tea's antimicrobial compounds, such as flavonoids and terpenes, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses that can cause eye infections, promoting healing and reducing the risk of complications.
remedy_parts: Roots: Used for their anti-inflammatory properties, which help soothe and calm irritated eyes.
Flowers: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, they provide a natural remedy for soothing dry, itchy, and tired eyes.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried calendula flowers containing no signs of mold or damage.
Measure one teaspoon of dried calendula flowers or two teaspoons of fresh calendula petals.
Pour boiling water over the measured calendula flowers and allow it to steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean cup discarding solids.
Let the tea cool down to room temperature before using it as an eye wash or compress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eyebright teas helps with sore eyes because its potent blend of herbs, such as Euphrasia officinalis, works to soothe and calm irritated eyes. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties reduce redness and swelling, while its antihistamine qualities combat itchiness and discharge. Additionally, eyebright's antioxidant-rich composition helps repair damaged eye tissue and promote healthy vision. By drinking eyebright tea regularly, individuals can experience a significant reduction in eye discomfort and enjoy improved visual clarity and comfort.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Eyebright teas contain anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids, terpenes, and glycosides that help reduce swelling and irritation in the eyes, providing relief from redness and discomfort.

Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in eyebright tea, such as beta-carotene and lutein, neutralize free radicals that can damage ocular tissues and contribute to dry eye syndrome, promoting healthy vision and reducing oxidative stress.

Antibacterial and antiviral activity: Eyebright's antibacterial and antiviral properties help combat infections and reduce the risk of complications from conjunctivitis (pink eye) and other eye infections, promoting a quick recovery.
remedy_parts: Roots: Eyebright roots are used to soothe and calm irritated eyes, reducing redness and inflammation.
Leaves: The leaves of the eyebright plant contain antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in treating bacterial infections and inflammation in the eyes.
remedy_recipe: Gather 15-20 grams of dried euphrasia officinalis flowers and store them in an airtight container for future use.
Measure one teaspoon of the dried euphrasia officinalis flowers and add it to a teapot or infuser cup containing boiling water.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes, releasing the herbal compounds into the water.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean cup, discarding the solids.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature, then sip it slowly and steadily as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree teas helps with sore eyes because of its unique combination of antioxidants, anti-inflammatory compounds, and soothing properties. The tea's high concentration of vitamin A and beta-carotene helps to reduce inflammation and irritation in the eyes, while its calming effects ease dryness and itchiness. Additionally, the tea's flavonoids and phenolic acids work together to protect the eyes from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals. As a result, drinking maidenhair tree teas can provide fast and effective relief for sore and tired eyes.
remedy_properties: Calendula tea: Has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help soothe and heal irritated or infected eyes.
Echinacea tea: Contains antioxidants and has immunomodulatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with eye infections or allergies.
Marshmallow root tea: Possesses soothing and demulcent properties, which can help calm and protect the mucous membranes in the eyes, providing relief from dryness, itchiness, or irritation.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain bioactive compounds that help reduce inflammation and ease discomfort in sore eyes.
Barks: The barks have anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe and calm irritated eyes, providing relief from redness and itchiness.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh ginkgo biloba leaves in the morning or evening when they are at their most potent.
Rinse 10-20 grams of dried ginkgo biloba leaves with cold water and drain well.
Steep the ginkgo biloba leaves in one quart of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding the solids immediately.
Drink the tea immediately while still warm, allowing the bioactive compounds to absorb effectively into the body.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with sore eyes because it has anti-inflammatory properties that reduce swelling and ease irritation. remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning or evening for optimal potency.
Rinse 1 teaspoon of dried taraxacum officinale herbs or 2 teaspoons of fresh herbs with filtered water to remove impurities.
Steep the herbal mixture in a teapot or infuser with 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release beneficial compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids, allowing the liquid to cool slightly.
Drink the warm or cooled taraxacum officinale tea within an hour after preparation to help soothe sore eyes and reduce inflammation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's Wort teas helps with sore eyes because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The tea contains hyperforin, a compound that reduces swelling and redness in the eyes. By soothing dry, irritated eyes, St John's Wort teas can provide quick relief from discomfort and promote a sense of calm.

Flavonoids: The flavonoids present in St John's Wort tea have antioxidant and antiviral properties, which can help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals and viruses that may contribute to eye infections or inflammation.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh st john's wort leaves and flowers from a clean source ensuring proper identification and handling.
Rinse the harvested plant material gently with cool water to remove any dirt or debris immediately.
Combine 10-15 grams of dried or 20-30 grams of fresh hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers in a heat-resistant tea infuser securely.
Steep the herb mixture in boiling water for 5-7 minutes allowing for optimal extraction of beneficial compounds thoroughly.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and discard solids serving hot or cooled according to personal preference instantly.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile teas helps with sore eyes because it contains a unique combination of anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The apigenin present in chamomile binds to GABA receptors in the brain, promoting relaxation and reducing eye tension. This calming effect soothes irritated eyes, reducing redness and itchiness. Additionally, chamomile's antiseptic properties help combat infections that may be contributing to sore eyes, allowing for quicker recovery and reduced discomfort. Overall, herbal chamomile teas provide a natural solution for soothing and calming sore eyes.

Soothing apigenin: Apigenin, a key compound in chamomile, has been shown to have a sedative effect on the body's nervous system, helping to calm irritated eyes and reduce sensitivity to light.

Roots: The roots of the chamomile plant contain apigenin, an antioxidant that helps reduce redness and inflammation in the eyes.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers into a heat-resistant cup.
Bring 1 cup of boiling water to a boil in a separate pot.
Pour the boiling water over the chamomile flowers and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into another cup.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before using it as an eye wash or compress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Comfrey teas helps with sore eyes because it contains powerful anti-inflammatory compounds that soothe and calm irritated eye tissues. remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried symphytum officinale leaves in sufficient quantity for the desired dosage.
Measure one teaspoon of dried symphytum officinale leaves per cup of boiling water.
Steep the leaves in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.

Drink the tea warm or at room temperature three times a day as needed for symptom relief.

Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large bowl, discarding the solids and allowing the liquid to cool slightly.
Store the cooled verbena officinalis tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours before drinking, drinking 1 cup twice daily as needed for sore eyes relief.

Drink two to three cups of rosemary tea throughout the day as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme teas helps with sore eyes because it contains compounds that have natural anti-inflammatory properties. When brewed into a tea, thyme releases these beneficial compounds, which can be used as a soothing compress to alleviate redness and discomfort. The warm liquid also provides gentle moisture to help flush out irritants and promote healing. As the thyme tea is absorbed through the skin, it reduces swelling and calms irritated tissue, providing swift relief from sore eyes.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Thyme's high antioxidant content helps to neutralize free radicals that can cause inflammation and irritation in the eyes, reducing redness and discomfort.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Thyme contains compounds like thymol and carvacrol, which have anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce swelling and soothe irritated eyes.

Antimicrobial properties: Thyme's antimicrobial properties can help to combat bacterial or fungal infections that may be contributing to eye irritation, reducing the risk of infection and promoting healing.
remedy_parts: Roots: Thyme roots are a natural anti-inflammatory and antibacterial, making them effective in reducing redness and inflammation in the eyes.
Leaves: The leaves of thyme contain antioxidants and volatile oils that help soothe and calm irritated eye tissues.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh thymus vulgaris leaves from your garden or store-dried leaves in an airtight container.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried thymus vulgaris leaves per cup of boiling water.
Steep the thymus vulgaris leaves in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to allow active compounds to release.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean cup and discard solids.
Let the tea cool to room temperature before applying it as an eye compress or rinsing affected eyes.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat sore eyes with herbal teas is to steep calendula flowers in boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea and let it cool before applying a warm compress to the affected eye. Repeat this process several times a day to soothe dryness, redness, and itchiness. Another option is to use chamomile tea as an eyewash by soaking a clean cloth in the cooled tea and gently wiping around the eyes.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to sore eyes that are treated with herbal teas are conjunctivitis, sinusitis, and blepharitis. Herbs such as chamomile, calendula, and eyebright can help soothe and calm irritated eyes, reduce inflammation, and promote healing. These herbs may also be used to treat respiratory issues like coughs and colds that can cause eye discomfort. Drinking these herbal teas or using them as eye washes can provide natural relief from these ailments.
